Methodology White Paper
Version 1.0

Employment Composition (CPS)

Monthly composite of labor market health from CPS: Participation, Employmentâ€“Population Ratio, and Unemployment Rate. Robust zâ€‘scores, momentum diagnostics, 0â€“100 scaling, and categorical regimes.

Why: Higher participation and employment with lower unemployment indicate a tighter, healthier labor market; the inverse signals rising slack.

Abstract

We construct a monthly index from three CPS (household survey) series: Participation, Employmentâ€“Population, and Unemployment. Each is standardized with a robust rolling zâ€‘score. We then build a weighted composite (participation 0.4, empâ€‘pop 0.4, unemployment âˆ’0.2), apply smoothing, scale it to 0â€“100, and classify regimes.

1. Data (BLS CPS Identifiers)

  • LNS11300000 â€” Labor force participation rate (%)
  • LNS12300000 â€” Employmentâ€“population ratio (%)
  • LNS14000000 â€” Unemployment rate (%)

Inputs are monthly. If any required ID is missing from the initial subset, the pipeline augments from long_df (or many) before pivoting.

2. Data Handling & Validation

  • Types & dates: Coerce value to numeric; parse date to monthâ€‘start timestamps.
  • Pivot & grid: Wide pivot by series_id â†’ monthly start grid via asfreq('MS').
  • Gaps: Bounded forwardâ€‘fill ffill(limit=2); larger gaps remain NaN.
  • Failâ€‘fast: raise if any of the three core series is entirely missing after pivot.

3. Feature Engineering

Participation_vs_EmpRatio = Participation / EmpPop
Labor_Slack_Index = (1 âˆ’ Unemployment/10) Ã— Participation

Momentum diagnostics: 3â€‘month differences for each core rate (diff(3)).

4. Standardisation (Robust Rolling zâ€‘scores)

Each series is scaled with a median/MAD zâ€‘score using an adaptive window.

zt(x) = (xt âˆ’ medianW(x)) / (1.4826Â·MADW(x)),\ W = min(36, max(8, âŒŠ0.8Â·NvalidâŒ‹))

Unemployment enters negatively via unemp_z_neg = âˆ’z(unemployment).

5. Weighting & Composite Construction

Weights reflect economic intuition: participation and employment carry equal weight; unemployment detracts.

{
  "part_z": 0.40,
  "empr_z": 0.40,
  "unemp_z_neg": 0.20
}
Employment_Compositionz = 0.40Â·partz + 0.40Â·emprz + 0.20Â·unempz_neg

6. Smoothing, Scaling & Regimes

  • Headline smoothing: 3â€‘period EMA on the composite zâ€‘score.
  • Score (0â€“100): minâ€“max scaling over observed history; returns NaN if flat.
  • Regimes: thresholds on the unsmoothed composite zâ€‘score.
  • HOT (healthy/tight): > +0.75
  • NEUTRAL: âˆ’0.75 to +0.75
  • COOL (slack rising): < âˆ’0.75

7. Output Panel

[
  "participation_rate","emp_pop_ratio","unemployment_rate",
  "Participation_vs_EmpRatio","Labor_Slack_Index",
  "part_mom_3m","empr_mom_3m","unemp_mom_3m",
  "part_z","empr_z","unemp_z_neg",
  "Employment_Composition_z","Employment_Composition_0_100",
  "Employment_Composition_Smoothed","Employment_Regime"
]

8. Implementation Notes (Python)

# Expect columns: date, series_id, value
REQ = ["LNS11300000","LNS12300000","LNS14000000"]
# Merge in any missing IDs from long_df/many prior to pivot; align to MS grid; ffill(limit=2)
# Apply robust_z(), momentum, composite, EMA smoothing, regime, and 0â€“100 scale as specified above.

9. Interpretation & Use

A HOT employment composition reading aligns with historically tight labor conditions and potential wage pressure; a COOL reading indicates rising slack. Use alongside wage, inflation, and liquidity signals for crossâ€‘validation.

Â© 2025. Employment Composition signal specification.
